name: San Francisco
lifestyle: Global tech capital with concentrated dining, culture, and climate appeal, offset by the highest US state tax burden and extreme housing costs. California's 13.3% top rate plus CA's LTCG-as-ordinary treatment make exit planning materially important at Fat FIRE scale. Central 1BRs are compressed but walkable living is excellent.

true_fat_fire.description: Pacific Heights/Russian Hill 2-3BR luxury apartment, Michelin dining monthly, weekly housekeeping, 3+ international trips plus Napa weekends.

guide.groceries: Whole Foods, Bi-Rite, specialty wine, butcher, and farmers' market shopping with premium produce, seafood, and easy entertaining at home.
guide.dining: Several good dinners out most weeks plus regular tasting-menu or Michelin nights, with bigger dinners often around $175-$350 per person before wine.
guide.rhythm: Ride-hail or car service when useful, weekly housekeeping, boutique fitness or wellness, and a social calendar that includes Napa, Sonoma, and long-haul travel.

comfortable_expat.description: Mission/Hayes Valley/Noe Valley 1BR apartment, dining out 3 nights/week, Muni plus rideshares, 1-2 international trips plus wine country weekends.

guide.groceries: A mix of Trader Joe's, Whole Foods, Bi-Rite-style top-ups, and farmers' market produce with enough room for good proteins and pantry staples.
guide.dining: Dining out around 2-3 nights a week plus the occasional nicer dinner, often $30-$60 casually and $120-$220 per person when stepping up.
guide.rhythm: Mostly Muni, walking, and rideshares, regular personal care, and weekends that lean into wine country, coast, or cabin trips rather than full luxury logistics.

luxury_family.description: Noe Valley/Presidio Heights 4BR house, top private school access (Town/Hamlin/SF Day) when dependents apply, household help available, international plus ski trips annually.

guide.groceries: Big family grocery basket with premium produce, butcher or seafood counters, school lunches, pantry staples, and easy-hosting extras.
guide.dining: Family brunches and dinners out most weekends plus adult date nights, with casual family meals often $35-$70 per person and special dinners much higher.
guide.rhythm: Household help, dense school or activity logistics, and a calendar built around ski weeks, camps, and annual long-haul family travel.
